In fact, this is surely not correct.  This change reverts https://github.com/scipy/scipy/commit/e9eb6182d5d3297e79b432a66a2828eb720431ca, and uses `np.sign(np.sign(z) + 0.5)` to compute the complex sign of `z`.

Before numpy 2.0, `np.sign(z)` returned the sign of the real part of `z`.  So the expression `np.sign(np.sign(z) + 0.5)` would compute the sign of the real part of `z` unless `z` was 0, in which case it returns 1.  This is a different calculation than the one suggested in the paper by Higham and Tisseur, which is probably why the change in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/commit/e9eb6182d5d3297e79b432a66a2828eb720431ca was made.

In numpy 2.0, the behavior of `np.sign` was changed to compute, in effect, `z/|z|` (with z=0 handled to return 0).  So in numpy 2.0, the expression `np.sign(np.sign(z) + 0.5)` is not useful. For example, `u=0.6+0.8j` is on the unit circle, so `sign(u)` should equal `u`.  But `np.sign(np.sign(u) + 0.5)` returns the wrong value `0.8087360843031886+0.5881716976750462j`.  The existing function `sign_round_up()` returns the correct value.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2374101904/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

stats.percentileofscore: 2D array input should be fixed or forbidden","user":{"login":"nickodell","id":666402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY2NjQwMg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/666402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickodell","html_url":"https://github.com/nickodell","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickodell/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickodell/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickodell/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickodell/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickodell/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickodell/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickodell/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickodell/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nickodell/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":37340722,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MDcyMg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/scipy.stats","name":"scipy.stats","color":"0366d6","default":false,"description":""},{"id":37340735,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MDczNQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/enhancement","name":"enhancement","color":"bfe5bf","default":true,"description":"A new feature or improvement"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-09-15T18:02:07Z","updated_at":"2024-09-25T20:22:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

[percentileofscore](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.stats.percentileofscore.html) is documented to operate on 1D arrays. If one uses a 2D array for the `a` parameter, bizarre behavior happens.

```
import scipy
import numpy as np

a = np.array([
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
])
print(scipy.stats.percentileofscore(a, 5))
print(scipy.stats.percentileofscore(a.T, 5))
```

Output:

```
[500.]
[20. 20. 20. 20. 20.]
```

The first behavior does not make sense, because 500% is not a percentile. The second behavior does not make sense, because 5 ought to be at the 100% percentile, and I only provided one input, not five.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

This function is documented to only accept 1D arrays. Ideally it would do something sensible, but if that's not possible, the next best thing would be to forbid 2D input to the `a` parameter to this function. (Would this need to be phased in with a deprecation?)

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

It would be best if percentileofscore returned a sensible result in this situation. I am not sure what a sensible result is if the `a` parameter is 2D.

It may also be good to forbid 2D input to `score`, while we're here.

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

This originally occurred to a user on [StackOverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/78967500/why-would-scipy-stats-percentileofscore-return-an-array-instead-of-a-scaler-begi).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21563/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21563/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2375179244","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21563#issuecomment-2375179244","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21563","id":2375179244,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6Nklvs","user":{"login":"WarrenWeckesser","id":321463,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyMTQ2Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/321463?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser","html_url":"https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-25T20:22:03Z","updated_at":"2024-09-25T20:22:03Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"FYI: NumPy gufuncs implement broadcasting of vectors and higher dimensional arrays in a standard way; see ["Generalized universal function API"](https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/c-api/generalized-ufuncs.html).   This is what @mdhaber might have been referring to when he mentioned discussing this with @seberg.

The idea is that the function implements a *core* (or *elementary*) operation on arrays or scalars, and broadcasting is applied to the dimensions of the inputs that are not part of the core operation.

For `percentileofscore` (and considering just the first two parameters), the core operation takes a 1-d array `a` and a scalar `score` and returns a scalar (the percentile).  Shapewise, the signature for this core operation is describe with the notation `(n),()->()`.  Broadcasting of the inputs would be applied to the shapes after removing the core dimensions from their shapes.   For example, given inputs `a` and `score` with shapes `(5, 1000)` and `(10, 1)`, respectively, the first step is to remove the dimensions corresponding to the core operations, working from the right by default.  For `a`, the core dimension is `n=1000`, so the shape that takes part in broadcasting is `(5,)`.  For `score`, the core shape is a scalar, so no dimension is removed from the input shape, and `(10, 1)` is used for broadcasting.  The broadcast shape of `(5,)` and `(10, 1)` is `(10, 5)`, so that is the shape of the result.

One reason that gufuncs don't make it into public APIs very often is that--compared to the flexibility of the signature of a function defined with `def`--the gufunc signature is relatively primitive. Specifically, the core parameters are strictly positional only.  You can't pass them in with a keyword, and you can't provide a default value for any of them.  (One way to work around this is to provide a wrapper of the gufunc that provides a more flexible API.)

As a demonstration, I implemented `percentileofscore` as a gufunc in [`ufunclab`](https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser/ufunclab).  I included the parameters `a`, `score` and `kind`, so the shape signature is `(n),(),()->()`.  Because it is a gufunc, the API is not exactly the same as that of `scipy.stats.percentileofscore`.  In particular, I chose to implement `kind` as an integer parameter (effectively an enum) instead of a string, and I didn't include `nan_policy` (but it could be added).  You can see examples in the [`percentileofscore`](https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser/ufunclab?tab=readme-ov-file#percentileofscore) section of README.md at ufunclab.  The relevant part for this discussion is the demonstration of broadcasting; with a gufunc, all three parameters (including `kind`) take part in broadcasting in a standard way.  The simple case where both `a` and `score` are 1-d and `kind` is a scalar is an example of broadcasting:

```
In [27]: import numpy as np

In [28]: from ufunclab import percentileofscore, op

In [29]: percentileofscore([2, 3, 4, 5, 5, 5, 8, 10], [4.5, 5, 6], op.KIND_RANK)
Out[29]: array([37.5, 62.5, 75. ])
```

Here's an example of broadcasting all three parameters that I doubt anyone would ever need in practice:

```
In [32]: rng = np.random.default_rng(121263137472525314065)

In [33]: a = rng.integers(0, 20, size=(3, 100))

In [34]: score = np.array([[0], [1], [10], [12], [19]])

In [35]: kind = np.array([op.KIND_RANK, op.KIND_WEAK, op.KIND_STRICT, op.KIND_MEAN]).reshape((4, 1, 1))
```

`a` has shape `(3, 100)`, `score` has shape `(5, 1)` and `kind` has shape `(4, 1, 1)`.  `percentileofscore` has shape signature `(n),(),()->()`, so the broadcast result will have shape `(4, 5, 3)`.

```
In [36]: result = percentileofscore(a, score, kind)

In [37]: result.shape
Out[37]: (4, 5, 3)

In [38]: result
Out[38]: 
array([[[  4. ,   3.5,   1.5],
        [ 10.5,   9. ,   3.5],
        [ 54. ,  54. ,  49.5],
        [ 63. ,  63. ,  56.5],
        [ 99.5,  98.5,  96. ]],

       [[  7. ,   6. ,   2. ],
        [ 13. ,  11. ,   4. ],
        [ 56. ,  56. ,  51. ],
        [ 64. ,  64. ,  58. ],
        [100. , 100. , 100. ]],

       [[  0. ,   0. ,   0. ],
        [  7. ,   6. ,   2. ],
        [ 51. ,  51. ,  47. ],
        [ 61. ,  61. ,  54. ],
        [ 98. ,  96. ,  91. ]],

       [[  3.5,   3. ,   1. ],
        [ 10. ,   8.5,   3. ],
        [ 53.5,  53.5,  49. ],
        [ 62.5,  62.5,  56. ],
        [ 99. ,  98. ,  95.5]]])
```

Anyone interested in extending how SciPy functions handle broadcasting should become familiar with this convention, even if only to have common understandng  of "gufunc-style broadcasting", and not because everything should be converted to gufuncs!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2375179244/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

improvement"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2024-09-15T18:02:07Z","updated_at":"2024-09-25T23:27:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

[percentileofscore](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.stats.percentileofscore.html) is documented to operate on 1D arrays. If one uses a 2D array for the `a` parameter, bizarre behavior happens.

```
import scipy
import numpy as np

a = np.array([
    [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
])
print(scipy.stats.percentileofscore(a, 5))
print(scipy.stats.percentileofscore(a.T, 5))
```

Output:

```
[500.]
[20. 20. 20. 20. 20.]
```

The first behavior does not make sense, because 500% is not a percentile. The second behavior does not make sense, because 5 ought to be at the 100% percentile, and I only provided one input, not five.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

This function is documented to only accept 1D arrays. Ideally it would do something sensible, but if that's not possible, the next best thing would be to forbid 2D input to the `a` parameter to this function. (Would this need to be phased in with a deprecation?)

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

It would be best if percentileofscore returned a sensible result in this situation. I am not sure what a sensible result is if the `a` parameter is 2D.

It may also be good to forbid 2D input to `score`, while we're here.

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

This originally occurred to a user on [StackOverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/78967500/why-would-scipy-stats-percentileofscore-return-an-array-instead-of-a-scaler-begi).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21563/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21563/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2375442396","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21563#issuecomment-2375442396","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21563","id":2375442396,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6Nll_c","user":{"login":"WarrenWeckesser","id":321463,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyMTQ2Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/321463?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser","html_url":"https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-25T23:27:14Z","updated_at":"2024-09-25T23:27:14Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"`np.quantile` and `np.percentile` don't actually *broadcast* their arguments.  After removing the `axis` dimension from the first argument, they perform the calculation on (effectively) the Cartesian product of the second and first arguments.  So if the inputs have shapes, say `(p, q, r)` and `(m, n)`, and `axis=1`, the output shape is `(m, n, p, r)`.  In your example, the inputs have shape (2, 10), and (2,). You want the output to have shape (2,), but that does not appear to be possible with a single function call.

With gufunc-style broadcasting, you get the desired behavior:

```
In [111]: from ufunclab import percentileofscore, op, vnorm

In [112]: a
Out[112]: 
array([[ 8, 11,  5, 18,  0,  7, 18, 19, 14, 12],
       [11,  1, 10, 14, 14,  8, 14,  3,  4, 17]])

In [113]: percentileofscore(a, [18.5, 2], op.KIND_STRICT)
Out[113]: array([90., 10.])

In [114]: vnorm(a, [1, np.inf])
Out[114]: array([112.,  17.])
```
I included `vnorm`, a gufunc with shape signture `(n),()->()` that computes the vector norm, to show that it uses the same rules as `percentileofscore`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2375442396/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```
[500.]

```

### Describe the solution you'd like.

This originally occurred to a user on [StackOverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/78967500/why-would-scipy-stats-percentileofscore-return-an-array-instead-of-a-scaler-begi).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21563/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21563/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2375469044","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21563#issuecomment-2375469044","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21563","id":2375469044,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6Nlsf0","user":{"login":"WarrenWeckesser","id":321463,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyMTQ2Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/321463?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser","html_url":"https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-25T23:57:34Z","updated_at":"2024-09-25T23:57:34Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> Is this a different standard, or was this ad hoc?

I suspect it is ad hoc.  I think these functions predate [NEP 5](https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0005-generalized-ufuncs.html), where gufuncs were first proposed.  @seberg or @mhvk might know more, but it is likely that the creation of the API for these predates their involvement in NumPy.

> With your gufunc percentileofscore, suppose you want the Cartesian product. In that case, do you just make sure that the batch shapes are orthogonal? (That is, one batch shape is (2, 1) and the other is (1, 2).)

Yes, exactly. In the following, the first argument of `percentileofscore` has shape (2, 10) and the second has shape (2, 1).  After removing the core dimension of the first shape, the remaining shape is (2,), and (2,) broadcast with (2, 1)  results in an output shape of (2, 2):

```
In [135]: a
Out[135]: 
array([[ 8, 11,  5, 18,  0,  7, 18, 19, 14, 12],
       [11,  1, 10, 14, 14,  8, 14,  3,  4, 17]])

In [136]: percentileofscore(a, [[18.5], [2]], op.KIND_STRICT)
Out[136]: 
array([[ 90., 100.],
       [ 10.,  10.]])
```

Reference issue
Closes gh-14582
Supersedes gh-14590 (but preserves those commits)

#### What does this implement/fix?
gh-14582 reported:
> `scipy.special.spherical_jn` seems to generate nans for negative input arguments, which does not seem to be prohibited and the documentation does not seem to warn against.

gh-14590 implemented reflection formulas to fix this for `spherical_jn` and the other spherical Bessel functions.

This re-implements the reflection formulas for both the functions and their derivatives in Python (since the underlying Cython files were deleted) and simplifies the tests. I chose a non-invasive (decorator) approach so that the implementation is easy to remove when there is time to implement the reflection formulas in the underlying C++ files. I opened a new PR because I can't push to the original branch, but we'll make sure the original author gets commit credit. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27477","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27477/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27477/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27477/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27477","id":2556596181,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86YYo_V","number":27477,"title":"BUG: numpy.matrix is not supporting dot product","user":{"login":"JayMan91","id":45946200,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ1OTQ2MjAw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/45946200?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/JayMan91","html_url":"https://github.com/JayMan91","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JayMan91/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JayMan91/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JayMan91/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JayMan91/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JayMan91/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JayMan91/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JayMan91/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JayMan91/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/JayMan91/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-09-30T12:56:23Z","updated_at":"2024-09-30T14:42:13Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

I want to multiply each column of a matrix with a different number. I can do that with using `*` operator when the matrix is an `numpy.ndarray` object. But it is not supported if the matrix `numpy.matrix` object. To an end-user this is strange,
as `numpy.matrix`  objects are a subclass of `numpy.ndarray`  and are supposed to inherit all the attributes and methods of `numpy.ndarray` .

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

V = np.arange(-3,3).reshape(2,3)
s =  np.array([0., 1, -1])
# I want to multiply 3 columns of V with 0, 1 and -1 respectively
op =  V*s
# This works
# Now let's transform V into a matrix
V = np.asmatrix(V)
op =  V*s
# This throws an error
```


### Error message:

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/ipykernel_14275/2296465942.py", line 3, in <module>
    V * S
  File "/home/jayanta/Documents/Projects/SolverFreeDFL/pkg/env_dfl/lib/python3.10/site-packages/numpy/matrixlib/defmatrix.py", line 219, in __mul__
    return N.dot(self, asmatrix(other))
ValueError: shapes (2,3) and (1,3) not aligned: 3 (dim 1) != 1 (dim 0)
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

numpy version: 1.26.2
sys version: 3.10.12

### Runtime Environment:

_No response_

### Context for the issue:

`np.linalg.svd` returns `U, S, Vh` as `numpy.matrix` . Operations on `U, S, Vh`  without converting them into  `numpy.ndarray` is raising errors, which is frustrating. Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":959099844,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5NTkwOTk4NDQ=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.ufunc","name":"component: numpy.ufunc","color":"009800","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2024-06-17T16:09:35Z","updated_at":"2024-08-01T15:23:21Z","closed_at":"2024-08-01T15:23:21Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

When receiving infinity as a (theoretically illegal) argument, `gcd()` hangs instead of throwing an exception.
In this case, infinity is an element of an array of object dtype.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

inf = np.array([np.inf], dtype=np.object_)
gcd = np.gcd(inf, 33)
```


### Error message:

```shell
No error. Function `gcd()` remains in an infinite loop.
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Numpy: 1.26.4
Python: 3.10.12

When receiving infinity as a (theoretically illegal) argument, `gcd()` hangs instead of throwing an exception.
In this case, infinity is an element of an array of object dtype.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

inf = np.array([np.inf], dtype=np.object_)
gcd = np.gcd(inf, 33)
```


### Error message:

```shell
No error. Function `gcd()` remains in an infinite loop.
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Numpy: 1.26.4
Python: 3.10.12

Maintenance","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":81498520,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw4MTQ5ODUyMA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/07%20-%20Deprecation","name":"07 - Deprecation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-07-29T23:00:37Z","updated_at":"2024-08-02T11:32:16Z","closed_at":"2024-08-02T09:15:44Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27076","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27076","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27076.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27076.patch","merged_at":"2024-08-02T09:15:44Z"},"body":"The first argument of bincount is expected to contain integers. As noted in gh-3138, it actually accepts floats, and casts them to integers with no warnings. (It also accepts other objects that can be cast to integers, so inputs such as ["1", Fraction(5, 3)] are accepted, with fractional parts silently dropped.)

This change deprecates that behavior.  Now a deprecation warning is generated if the input cannot be safely cast to integer.

For example,

```
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: np.bincount([1.4, 1.5, 3.9, 5.1])
<ipython-input-2-6d924fc4fef0>:1: DeprecationWarning: Non-integer input passed to bincount. In a future version of NumPy, this will be an error.
  np.bincount([1.4, 1.5, 3.9, 5.1])
Out[2]: array([0, 2, 0, 1, 0, 1])
```

Closes gh-3138.

Describe your issue.

`stats.beta.ppf` incorrectly returns NaN with certain arguments in Scipy 1.14.0 on Ubuntu when installed from the wheel.

The issue appears with multiple versions of Ubuntu (20.04 and 22.04), Numpy (1.24, 1.25, 1.26, 2.0), and Python (3.10, 3.11, 3.12).

The code below demonstrates the issue. This same code does not produce the bug on Mac OS or in earlier versions of Scipy; thus, the issue seems specific to the manylinux wheel for Scipy 1.14.0.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy import stats

stats.beta(5., 5.).ppf(0.5)
```


### Error message

```shell
np.float64(nan)
```

In https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20393 (ping @steppi), the line

```
#define BOOST_MATH_PROMOTE_DOUBLE_POLICY      false
```

was removed from `scipy/special/boost_special_functions.h` and replaced by more policy-related typedefs a few lines further down in the file.  If I restore that line before all the includes from boost/math, `beta(5.0, 5.0).ppf(0.5)` returns 0.5.  So it looks like the newer code ends up not setting the policy as expected.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2267272496/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```

[[0]
 [0]
 [0]
 [0]
 [0]]

print("\nSorted data:")

```

[[[0.12345678]]
 [[0.12345678]]
 [[0.12345678]]
 [[0.12345678]]
 [[0.12345678]]]
```

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

# Create a sample array (a 1D NumPy array) where each element is an array of one element each, with values between 0 and 1, to at least 8 decimal places, without scientific notation
data = np.array([[0.12345678], [0.90123456], [0.98765432], [0.00001234], [0.56789012]])

print("Original data:")
print(data)

# Use argsort to get the indices of sorted array
indices = np.argsort(data)

print("\nSorted indices:")
print(indices)

# Now you can use these indices to sort your original array
sorted_data = data[indices]
print("\nSorted data:")
print(sorted_data)
```

```shell
No error message
```

Python == 3.12.4

By default `argsort` acts along the *last* axis of the input array.  In your case, `argsort` is applied to each (trivial) row, and since each row contains just one element, the result for each row is `[0]`.

Either add the argument `axis=0` (e.g. `argsort(data, axis=0)`) to apply `argsort` to each column (which means just the one column that you have), or flatten the input (e.g. `argsort(data.ravel())`) so that `argsort` is applied to all the data, treated as a 1-d array.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2267289315/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Describe your issue.

`stats.beta.ppf` incorrectly returns NaN with certain arguments in Scipy 1.14.0 on Ubuntu when installed from the wheel.

The issue appears with multiple versions of Ubuntu (20.04 and 22.04), Numpy (1.24, 1.25, 1.26, 2.0), and Python (3.10, 3.11, 3.12).

The code below demonstrates the issue. This same code does not produce the bug on Mac OS or in earlier versions of Scipy; thus, the issue seems specific to the manylinux wheel for Scipy 1.14.0.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy import stats

stats.beta(5., 5.).ppf(0.5)
```


### Error message

```shell
np.float64(nan)
```

Closes gh-21303

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR fixes `stats.beta.ppf` for the case `stats.beta(5., 5.).ppf(0.5)` by adjusting the Boost policies as suggested by @WarrenWeckesser here, https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21303#issuecomment-2267563656.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
Thanks @WarrenWeckesser for investigating this and opening an issue upstream, https://github.com/boostorg/math/issues/1169. It's still not entirely clear to me what the underlying issue is and why the policies to be set this way, but I've verified that the fix works.

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR fixes `stats.beta.ppf` for the case `stats.beta(5., 5.).ppf(0.5)` by adjusting the Boost policies as suggested by @WarrenWeckesser here, https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21303#issuecomment-2267563656.

<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
Thanks @WarrenWeckesser for investigating this and opening an issue upstream, https://github.com/boostorg/math/issues/1169. It's still not entirely clear to me what the underlying issue is and why the policies need to be set this way, but I've verified that the fix works.

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
Closes gh-21303

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR fixes `stats.beta.ppf` for the case `stats.beta(5., 5.).ppf(0.5)` by adjusting the Boost policies as suggested by @WarrenWeckesser here, https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21303#issuecomment-2267563656.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
Thanks @WarrenWeckesser for investigating this and opening an issue upstream, https://github.com/boostorg/math/issues/1169. It's still not entirely clear to me what the underlying issue is and why the policies need to be set this way, but I've verified that the fix works.

Describe your issue.

`stats.beta.ppf` incorrectly returns NaN with certain arguments in Scipy 1.14.0 on Ubuntu when installed from the wheel.

The issue appears with multiple versions of Ubuntu (20.04 and 22.04), Numpy (1.24, 1.25, 1.26, 2.0), and Python (3.10, 3.11, 3.12).

The code below demonstrates the issue. This same code does not produce the bug on Mac OS or in earlier versions of Scipy; thus, the issue seems specific to the manylinux wheel for Scipy 1.14.0.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy import stats

stats.beta(5., 5.).ppf(0.5)
```


### Error message

```shell
np.float64(nan)
```

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
Closes gh-21303

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR fixes `stats.beta.ppf` for the case `stats.beta(5., 5.).ppf(0.5)` by adjusting the Boost policies as suggested by @WarrenWeckesser here, https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21303#issuecomment-2267563656.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
Thanks @WarrenWeckesser for investigating this and opening an issue upstream, https://github.com/boostorg/math/issues/1169. It's still not entirely clear to me what the underlying issue is and why the policies need to be set this way, but I've verified that the fix works.

this description has been edited.*

The loss of precision of `t.logcdf` was noted in a [stackoverflow question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/78822823/are-there-numerically-stable-python-implementations-of-log-cumulative-distributi/); this change implements the idea from [my answer](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/78822823/are-there-numerically-stable-python-implementations-of-log-cumulative-distributi/78823595#78823595) generically as the `_logcdf` and `_logsf` methods of `rv_continuous`.

The change affects those distributions that do not already override `_logcdf` or `_logsf`.  The method is most effective for distributions that override `_sf` with a method that computes the survival function with good precision in the right tail. Explicit tests for values from the tails of the distributions, with reference values computed with mpmath, are added for the distributions  `beta`, `betaprime`, `exponweib`, `gamma`, `gompertz`, `halflogistic`, `hypsecant`, `invgamma`, `laplace`, `levy`, `loggamma`, `maxwell`, `nakagami`, and `t`.  That does not cover all the affected distributions, but I think it demonstrates that the method works well.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21301/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21301/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2272114965","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21301#issuecomment-2272114965","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21301","id":2272114965,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6HbbkV","user":{"login":"WarrenWeckesser","id":321463,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyMTQ2Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/321463?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser","html_url":"https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-06T20:43:00Z","updated_at":"2024-08-06T20:43:00Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I have updated the pull request to implement `_logcdf` and `_logsf` generically in the `rv_continuous` class--see the updated description of the PR above.

improvement"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":10,"created_at":"2024-05-22T08:32:03Z","updated_at":"2024-08-07T15:09:29Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

I have found that there is a problem with calculation accuracy in the tail of the Cauchy distribution.

https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/4edfcaa3ce8a387450b6efce968572def71be089/scipy/stats/_continuous_distns.py#L1382-L1392

I would like to see this improved based on boost implementation.
https://www.boost.org/doc/libs/1_85_0/libs/math/doc/html/math_toolkit/dist_ref/dists/cauchy_dist.html

### Describe the solution you'd like.

_No response_","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/20761/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/20761/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2273704416","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/20761#issuecomment-2273704416","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/20761","id":2273704416,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6Hhfng","user":{"login":"WarrenWeckesser","id":321463,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyMTQ2Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/321463?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser","html_url":"https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-07T15:09:29Z","updated_at":"2024-08-07T15:09:29Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I found that the standard form (i.e. loc=0, scale=1) of the CDF and SF for the Cauchy distribution can be written as follows:

```
def cauchy_cdf(x):
    return np.arctan2(1, -x)/np.pi


def cauchy_sf(x):
    return np.arctan2(1, x)/np.pi
```

These give good precision over the entire domain.

The inverses can be implemented by wrapping the functions available in Boost.

I'll submit a PR soon with these updates.

(This wouldn't matter in `_cauchy_ppf` anyway, since we only use this private function in a place where `p` has already been validated.)  I'm note sure why that use of `p - floor(p)` is there; @jzmaddock, is that needed? Also note that it computes `-scale / tan(constants::pi<RealType>() * P)`, where `P` was computed above; `P` is not `p`.  If you take a look at the tests that I include in the PR, you'll see that the precision is good.  Did you find a case where it is not?

The Boost code *is* relatively simple in this case, but will still require conditionals if we implement the same method as the Boost code, which means it should really be implemented as a ufunc in a compiled language.  So we might as well use the existing code in Boost.

If there are any problems in the Boost code, we can create an issue for it at the boost/math github page.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2274553199/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

#### What does this implement/fix?
There is a test that checks whether distribution methods accept lists as arguments. The continuous version of the test looks OK, but the discrete version of the test did not test all the methods it intended to. This fixes the test. 

#### Additional information
A test failed, exposing that `_logpmf` does not silence `divide` warnings. Eliminate spurious overflow warnings from `cauchy.pdf(x)` when |x| > 1.34e154 (i.e. `sqrt(np.finfo(np.float64).max)`).
* Implement `cauchy._logpdf` to extend the input range over which `cauchy.logpdf` gives correct results.  Before this change, `cauchy.logpdf(x)` would return -inf for |x| > 1.34e154.
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21347/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21347/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2278505620","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21347#issuecomment-2278505620","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21347","id":2278505620,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6HzzyU","user":{"login":"WarrenWeckesser","id":321463,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyMTQ2Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/321463?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser","html_url":"https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-09T18:23:49Z","updated_at":"2024-08-09T18:23:49Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> One question: assuming x > 0, does the expression np.log(x) + np.log(1/x + x) introduce (non-negligible) cancellation error for small x?

Yes it does! Cancellation occurs when |x| < 1, and the amount of cancellation increases as |x| → 0.  I was concerned about that, but my initial tests showed that the precision was pretty good, despite the reliance on cancellation to compute the result.  One reason, as you noted, is that for small enough x, the absolute error of the difference of the logs is small enough that the overall relative error of the sum when `_LOG_PI` is included is small.

Still, your question prompted me to experiment a bit more, and I found that, although the expression that I used wasn't bad, better precision can be obtained for |x| < 1 by using `-_LOG_PI - np.log1p(absx**2)`.  This can't be used for large |x| because of the possibility of overflow.  So I rewrote the method, begrudgingly using `_lazywhere`; in the tradeoff between precision and speed, I'm leaning towards precision here.  For |x| > 1, I changed the expression to `-_LOG_PI - (2*np.log(absx) + np.log1p(1/absx**2))`, because here, too, I found this version has slightly (but only very slightly) better precision than what I originally used.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/pulls/comments/1712663443","pull_request_review_id":2231467215,"id":1712663443,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABZIoc5mFSuT","diff_hunk":"@@ -1451,7 +1451,26 @@ def _shape_info(self):
 
     def _pdf(self, x):
         # cauchy.pdf(x) = 1 / (pi * (1 + x**2))
-        return 1.0/np.pi/(1.0+x*x)
+        with np.errstate(over='ignore'):
+            return 1.0/np.pi/(1.0+x*x)
+
+    def _logpdf(self, x):
+        # The formulas
+        #     log(1/(pi*(1 + x**2))) = -log(pi) - log(1 + x**2)
+        #                            = -log(pi) - log(x**2*(1 + 1/x**2))
+        #                            = -log(pi) - (2log(|x|) + log1p(1/x**2))
+        # are used here.
+        absx = np.abs(x)
+        # In the following _lazywhere, `f` provides better precision than `f2`
+        # for small and moderate x (although `f2` isn't too bad), while `f2`
+        # avoids the overflow that can occur with absx**2. An overflow of absx**2
+        # in the expression 1/absx**2 is not a problem, so we can ignore it.
+        with np.errstate(over='ignore'):
+            y = _lazywhere(absx < 1, (absx,),
+                           f=lambda absx: -_LOG_PI - np.log1p(absx**2),
+                           f2=lambda absx: (-_LOG_PI -
+                                            (2*np.log(absx) + np.log1p(1/absx**2))))","path":"scipy/stats/_continuous_distns.py","commit_id":"414d1214cd0c51ed5d61deb4a822642998a08160","original_commit_id":"ceb09aae256b4a1a6eb96d5dd05f76f90256b88a","user":{"login":"WarrenWeckesser","id":321463,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyMTQ2Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/321463?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser","html_url":"https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Yup, good idea. fixes","user":{"login":"WarrenWeckesser","id":321463,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyMTQ2Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/321463?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser","html_url":"https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"* Eliminate spurious overflow warnings from `cauchy.pdf(x)` when |x| > 1.34e154 (i.e. `sqrt(np.finfo(np.float64).max)`).
* Implement `cauchy._logpdf` to extend the input range over which `cauchy.logpdf` gives correct results.  Before this change, `cauchy.logpdf(x)` would return -inf for |x| > 1.34e154.

Some of these are small test issues; tolerance being slightly exceeded for a couple of `special` functions. Others indicate problems that need investigating and fixing. A good way of doing this is probably to look at the full set of flags with which each extension is compiled. These tests are not failing in `numpy.distutils` based CI, so the difference in build flags (e.g. `-std=c99` enabled, or optimization level) is likely the explanation.

Azure"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-08-11T21:09:12Z","updated_at":"2024-08-11T21:09:12Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The CI job `Windows tests / no pythran & sdist+wheel, full, py3.11/npPre, pip+pytest` on github actions has been failing on all pull requests for a while.

The problem appears to be that some of the ufuncs in `special` have been defined to accept an argument of type `long`, which is 32 bit on Windows, but the default integer type on 64 bit Windows is now 64 bit in NumPy 2.0.  So calling those ufuncs with an array of integers with the default integer type results in an error because they can't be safely cast to `long`.

The correct solution is to update all the ufuncs to use type `npy_intp` instead of `long`; see https://numpy.org/devdocs/numpy_2_0_migration_guide.html#windows-default-integer.  This doesn't look like a simple search-and-replace operation.  It will probably involve making some updates to the ufunc code generation tool `scipy/special/_generate_pyx.py` to include the `npy_intp` type as an option.

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->

Filed as suggested in [gh-21349 (comment)](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21349#issuecomment-2282896549).

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->

This PR removes the single-line if-then-endif constructs in `fpchec.f` that were causing syntactical errors when compiling with `f2c` as part of building SciPy with the Emscripten toolchain, possibly because `fpchec` uses some dated, punch-card FORTRAN syntax. It converts them to statements split over multiple lines.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->

This PR is part of a list of patches being upstreamed to Pyodide from compiling SciPy, which was recently updated to v1.13.0 in-tree: pyodide/pyodide#4719. This way, the patch can be removed in-tree if this code gets refactored/rewritten in C in a later version of SciPy or simply when SciPy is being updated to v1.15.0 if this file is left untouched after this gets Describe your issue.

When λ > 0, the support of the Tukey lambda distribution is (-1/λ, 1/λ).  Currently, `tukeylambda.support(lam)` returns (-inf, inf) even when `lam` > 0.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
In [1]: from scipy.stats import tukeylambda

In [2]: tukeylambda.support(0.5)  # Should be (-2, 2)
Out[2]: (np.float64(-inf), np.float64(inf))
```


### Error message

There is an error related to this problem.  The `pdf` method will generate a spurious warning when evaluated at the bounds of the support when `lam` > 0, e.g.

```
In [3]: tukeylambda.pdf(2, 0.5)
/home/warren/repos/git/forks/scipy/build-install/lib/python3.12/site-packages/scipy/stats/_continuous_distns.py:10492: RuntimeWarning: divide by zero encountered in power
  Px = Fx**(lam-1.0) + (np.asarray(1-Fx))**(lam-1.0)
Out[3]: np.float64(0.0)
```

Describe your issue.

When `lam` < 0, as `x` gets larger, `tukeylambda.cdf(x, lam)` saturates at `0.9999999999999929` instead of `1`, and `tukeylambda.sf(x, lam)` levels off at `7.105427357601002e-15` instead of decreasing to `0`.

The source of the problem is in the bisection method that used in `special.tklmbda`, which can be found in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/scipy/special/xsf/cephes/tukey.h.  That code needs to be refined, and we also need a corresponding function to compute the survival funciton.


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
In [6]: tukeylambda.cdf([1e4, 1e6, 1e8, 1e10, 1e12, 1e24], -0.5).tolist()
Out[6]: 
[0.999999960015991,
 0.9999999999959996,
 0.9999999999999929,
 0.9999999999999929,
 0.9999999999999929,
 0.9999999999999929]

In [7]: tukeylambda.sf([1e4, 1e6, 1e8, 1e10, 1e12, 1e24], -0.5).tolist()
Out[7]: 
[3.998400899263288e-08,
 4.000355602329364e-12,
 7.105427357601002e-15,
 7.105427357601002e-15,
 7.105427357601002e-15,
 7.105427357601002e-15]
```


### Error message

```shell
No error message.
```

NumPy tried to do `#undef I` in their headers, but that breaks downstream code that intentionally uses `I` from `complex.h` (and hacking definitions of standard headers is just bad practice).

Describe your issue.

```
python -c "import scipy.special, numpy as np;print(scipy.special.eval_hermite(np.arange(2), np.zeros((1,2), dtype=int)))"
  
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: ufunc 'eval_hermite' not supported for the input types, and the inputs could not be safely coerced to any supported types according to the casting rule ''safe''
```

One can create a GitHub Actions workflow to validate:

```yml
name: Test

on:
  push:

jobs:
  test:
    name: test scipy
    runs-on: windows-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
      with:
        python-version: "3.x"
    - run: pip install scipy
    - run: python -c "import scipy.special, numpy as np;print(scipy.special.eval_hermite(np.arange(2), np.zeros((1,2), dtype=int)))"
```

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import scipy.special, numpy as np
print(scipy.special.eval_hermite(np.arange(2), np.zeros((1,2), dtype=int)))
```


### Error message

```shell
TypeError: ufunc 'eval_hermite' not supported for the input types, and the inputs could not be safely coerced to any supported types according to the casting rule ''safe''
```


### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
scipy 1.14.0
numpy 2.0.0
python 3.12.4
Microsoft Windows Server 2022
```

Describe your issue.

When `lam` < 0, as `x` gets larger, `tukeylambda.cdf(x, lam)` saturates at `0.9999999999999929` instead of `1` and  `tukeylambda.sf(x, lam)` levels off at `7.105427357601002e-15` instead of decreasing to `0`. `tukeylambda.pdf(x, lam)` levels off at `5.9894274089194295e-22` in the postive and negative tails.

The source of the problem is in the bisection method that is used in `special.tklmbda`, which can be found in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/scipy/special/xsf/cephes/tukey.h.  That code needs to be refined.


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
In [6]: tukeylambda.cdf([1e4, 1e6, 1e8, 1e10, 1e12, 1e24], -0.5).tolist()
Out[6]: 
[0.999999960015991,
 0.9999999999959996,
 0.9999999999999929,
 0.9999999999999929,
 0.9999999999999929,
 0.9999999999999929]

In [7]: tukeylambda.sf([1e4, 1e6, 1e8, 1e10, 1e12, 1e24], -0.5).tolist()
Out[7]: 
[3.998400899263288e-08,
 4.000355602329364e-12,
 7.105427357601002e-15,
 7.105427357601002e-15,
 7.105427357601002e-15,
 7.105427357601002e-15]

In [8]: tukeylambda.pdf([1e4, 1e6, 1e8, 1e10, 1e12, 1e24], -0.5).tolist()
Out[8]: 
[7.995203177218486e-12,
 8.001066830697682e-18,
 5.9894274089194295e-22,
 5.9894274089194295e-22,
 5.9894274089194295e-22,
 5.9894274089194295e-22]
```

I refactored the tests of `logit`, and updated the reference values with values computed with mpmath.

Here's a script that uses mpmath to plot the relative error of logit over the interval (0, 1):

```python
from mpmath import mp
import numpy as np
import scipy
from scipy.special import logit
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


mp.dps = 200

p = np.linspace(0, 1, 8000)[1:-1]
y = logit(p)
ref = np.array([float(mp.log(t) - mp.log1p(-t)) for t in p])
relerr = abs((y - ref)/ref)

plt.plot(p, relerr)
plt.grid()
plt.xlabel('p')
plt.title('Relative error of scipy.special.logit')

# plt.show()
plt.savefig(f'logit-relerr-{scipy.__version__}.png')
```
With the main development branch, the plot is

![logit-relerr-1 15 0 dev0+1389 bfaef27](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5cddde1f-47eb-40b2-9e27-0a416546fe52)

With this PR, the plot is

![logit-relerr-1 15 0 dev0+1390 4d28195](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/57b41d70-53e3-4d70-90b0-02bfdaa82486)

 template <typename T>
 T logit(T x) {
-    return std::log(x / (1 - x));
+    // The standard formula is log(x/(1 - x)), but this expression
+    // loses precision near x=0.5, as does log(x) - log1p(-x).
+    // We use the latter just for small x, and otherwise use
+    // log1p(2*(x - 0.5)) - log1p(-2*(x - 0.5)), which provides
+    // very good precision for all x outside of an interval near 0.
+    if (x < 0.25) {

special.logit.","user":{"login":"WarrenWeckesser","id":321463,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyMTQ2Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/321463?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser","html_url":"https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Another "low hanging fruit" precision improvement: use formulations of `log(p/(1 - p))` that avoid loss of precision.

I refactored the tests of `logit`, and updated the reference values with values computed with mpmath.

Here's a script that uses mpmath to plot the relative error of logit over the interval (0, 1):

```python
from mpmath import mp
import numpy as np
import scipy
from scipy.special import logit
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


mp.dps = 200

p = np.linspace(0, 1, 8000)[1:-1]
y = logit(p)
ref = np.array([float(mp.log(t) - mp.log1p(-t)) for t in p])
relerr = abs((y - ref)/ref)

plt.plot(p, relerr)
plt.grid()
plt.xlabel('p')
plt.title('Relative error of scipy.special.logit')

# plt.show()
plt.savefig(f'logit-relerr-{scipy.__version__}.png')
```
With the main development branch, the plot shows the spike in the relative error around p = 0.5:

![logit-relerr-1 15 0 dev0+1389 bfaef27](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5cddde1f-47eb-40b2-9e27-0a416546fe52)

With this PR, the relative error for this sample is less than 2.5e-16 over the entire interval:

![logit-relerr-1 15 0 dev0+1392 779bb9b](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b6c2e51e-e0ef-4a51-8ff9-ac8025f48f54)

special.logit.","user":{"login":"WarrenWeckesser","id":321463,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyMTQ2Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/321463?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser","html_url":"https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Another "low hanging fruit" precision improvement: use formulations of `log(p/(1 - p))` that avoid loss of precision.

I refactored the tests of `logit`, and updated the reference values with values computed with mpmath.

Here's a script that uses mpmath to plot the relative error of logit over the interval (0, 1):

```python
from mpmath import mp
import numpy as np
import scipy
from scipy.special import logit
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


mp.dps = 200

p = np.linspace(0, 1, 8000)[1:-1]
y = logit(p)
ref = np.array([float(mp.log(t) - mp.log1p(-t)) for t in p])
relerr = abs((y - ref)/ref)

plt.plot(p, relerr)
plt.grid()
plt.xlabel('p')
plt.title('Relative error of scipy.special.logit')

# plt.show()
plt.savefig(f'logit-relerr-{scipy.__version__}.png')
```
With the main development branch, the plot shows the spike in the relative error around p = 0.5:

![logit-relerr-1 15 0 dev0+1389 bfaef27](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5cddde1f-47eb-40b2-9e27-0a416546fe52)

I refactored the tests of `logit`, and updated the reference values with values computed with mpmath.

Here's a script that uses mpmath to plot the relative error of logit over the interval (0, 1):

```python
from mpmath import mp
import numpy as np
import scipy
from scipy.special import logit
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


mp.dps = 200

p = np.linspace(0, 1, 8000)[1:-1]
y = logit(p)
ref = np.array([float(mp.log(t) - mp.log1p(-t)) for t in p])
relerr = abs((y - ref)/ref)

plt.plot(p, relerr)
plt.grid()
plt.xlabel('p')
plt.title('Relative error of scipy.special.logit')

# plt.show()
plt.savefig(f'logit-relerr-{scipy.__version__}.png')
```
With the main development branch, the plot shows the spike in the relative error around p = 0.5:

![logit-relerr-1 15 0 dev0+1389 bfaef27](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5cddde1f-47eb-40b2-9e27-0a416546fe52)

With this PR, the relative error for this sample is less than 2.5e-16 over the entire interval:

![logit-relerr-1 15 0 dev0+1392 779bb9b](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b6c2e51e-e0ef-4a51-8ff9-ac8025f48f54)

I refactored the tests of `logit`, and updated the reference values with values computed with mpmath.

Here's a script that uses mpmath to plot the relative error of logit over the interval (0, 1):

```python
from mpmath import mp
import numpy as np
import scipy
from scipy.special import logit
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


mp.dps = 200

p = np.linspace(0, 1, 8000)[1:-1]
y = logit(p)
ref = np.array([float(mp.log(t) - mp.log1p(-t)) for t in p])
relerr = abs((y - ref)/ref)

plt.plot(p, relerr)
plt.grid()
plt.xlabel('p')
plt.title('Relative error of scipy.special.logit')

# plt.show()
plt.savefig(f'logit-relerr-{scipy.__version__}.png')
```
With the main development branch, the plot shows the spike in the relative error around p = 0.5:

![logit-relerr-1 15 0 dev0+1389 bfaef27](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5cddde1f-47eb-40b2-9e27-0a416546fe52)

With this PR, the relative error for this sample is less than 2.5e-16 over the entire interval:

![logit-relerr-1 15 0 dev0+1392 779bb9b](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b6c2e51e-e0ef-4a51-8ff9-ac8025f48f54)

Describe your issue.

When `lam` < 0, as `x` gets larger, `tukeylambda.cdf(x, lam)` saturates at `0.9999999999999929` instead of `1` and  `tukeylambda.sf(x, lam)` levels off at `7.105427357601002e-15` instead of decreasing to `0`. `tukeylambda.pdf(x, lam)` levels off at `5.9894274089194295e-22` in the postive and negative tails.

The source of the problem is in the bisection method that is used in `special.tklmbda`, which can be found in https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/scipy/special/xsf/cephes/tukey.h.  That code needs to be refined.


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
In [6]: tukeylambda.cdf([1e4, 1e6, 1e8, 1e10, 1e12, 1e24], -0.5).tolist()
Out[6]: 
[0.999999960015991,
 0.9999999999959996,
 0.9999999999999929,
 0.9999999999999929,
 0.9999999999999929,
 0.9999999999999929]

In [7]: tukeylambda.sf([1e4, 1e6, 1e8, 1e10, 1e12, 1e24], -0.5).tolist()
Out[7]: 
[3.998400899263288e-08,
 4.000355602329364e-12,
 7.105427357601002e-15,
 7.105427357601002e-15,
 7.105427357601002e-15,
 7.105427357601002e-15]

In [8]: tukeylambda.pdf([1e4, 1e6, 1e8, 1e10, 1e12, 1e24], -0.5).tolist()
Out[8]: 
[7.995203177218486e-12,
 8.001066830697682e-18,
 5.9894274089194295e-22,
 5.9894274089194295e-22,
 5.9894274089194295e-22,
 5.9894274089194295e-22]
```

"low hanging fruit" precision improvement: use formulations of `log(p/(1 - p))` that avoid loss of precision.

I refactored the tests of `logit`, and updated the reference values with values computed with mpmath.

Here's a script that uses mpmath to plot the relative error of logit over the interval (0, 1):

```python
from mpmath import mp
import numpy as np
import scipy
from scipy.special import logit
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


mp.dps = 200

p = np.linspace(0, 1, 8000)[1:-1]
y = logit(p)
ref = np.array([float(mp.log(t) - mp.log1p(-t)) for t in p])
relerr = abs((y - ref)/ref)

plt.plot(p, relerr)
plt.grid()
plt.xlabel('p')
plt.title('Relative error of scipy.special.logit')

# plt.show()
plt.savefig(f'logit-relerr-{scipy.__version__}.png')
```
With the main development branch, the plot shows the spike in the relative error around p = 0.5:

![logit-relerr-1 15 0 dev0+1389 bfaef27](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5cddde1f-47eb-40b2-9e27-0a416546fe52)

With this PR, the relative error for this sample is less than 2.5e-16 over the entire interval:

![logit-relerr-1 15 0 dev0+1392 779bb9b](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b6c2e51e-e0ef-4a51-8ff9-ac8025f48f54)

special.logit.","user":{"login":"WarrenWeckesser","id":321463,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyMTQ2Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/321463?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser","html_url":"https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Another "low hanging fruit" precision improvement: use formulations of `log(p/(1 - p))` that avoid loss of precision.

I refactored the tests of `logit`, and updated the reference values with values computed with mpmath.

Here's a script that uses mpmath to plot the relative error of logit over the interval (0, 1):

```python
from mpmath import mp
import numpy as np
import scipy
from scipy.special import logit
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


mp.dps = 200

p = np.linspace(0, 1, 8000)[1:-1]
y = logit(p)
ref = np.array([float(mp.log(t) - mp.log1p(-t)) for t in p])
relerr = abs((y - ref)/ref)

plt.plot(p, relerr)
plt.grid()
plt.xlabel('p')
plt.title('Relative error of scipy.special.logit')

# plt.show()
plt.savefig(f'logit-relerr-{scipy.__version__}.png')
```
With the main development branch, the plot shows the spike in the relative error around p = 0.5:

![logit-relerr-1 15 0 dev0+1389 bfaef27](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5cddde1f-47eb-40b2-9e27-0a416546fe52)

With this PR, the relative error for this sample is less than 2.5e-16 over the entire interval:

![logit-relerr-1 15 0 dev0+1392 779bb9b](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b6c2e51e-e0ef-4a51-8ff9-ac8025f48f54)

Describe your issue.

```
python -c "import scipy.special, numpy as np;print(scipy.special.eval_hermite(np.arange(2), np.zeros((1,2), dtype=int)))"
  
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: ufunc 'eval_hermite' not supported for the input types, and the inputs could not be safely coerced to any supported types according to the casting rule ''safe''
```

One can create a GitHub Actions workflow to validate:

```yml
name: Test

on:
  push:

jobs:
  test:
    name: test scipy
    runs-on: windows-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
      with:
        python-version: "3.x"
    - run: pip install scipy
    - run: python -c "import scipy.special, numpy as np;print(scipy.special.eval_hermite(np.arange(2), np.zeros((1,2), dtype=int)))"
```

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import scipy.special, numpy as np
print(scipy.special.eval_hermite(np.arange(2), np.zeros((1,2), dtype=int)))
```


### Error message

```shell
TypeError: ufunc 'eval_hermite' not supported for the input types, and the inputs could not be safely coerced to any supported types according to the casting rule ''safe''
```


### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
scipy 1.14.0
numpy 2.0.0
python 3.12.4
Microsoft Windows Server 2022
```

T00:26:29Z","updated_at":"2024-08-15T22:10:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"It looks like most of the internal links within the SciPy API reference page include a link to an id within the page using a `#` reference that skips the heading of the page.  This means information that I would expect to see is not visible unless I scroll up to see the heading.

For example, start at the [`SciPy API`](https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/reference/index.html) page, and scroll down to the list of the subpackages, and click on the the `scipy.signal` link.  The URL for that link is actually `https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/reference/signal.html#module-scipy.signal`; note the internal id `#module-scipy.signal`.  Here's a screen shot of that page (using Firefox 96.0.3):

![scipy signal](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/321463/152662985-b2dc72be-039a-4a90-9ccd-4e539a218790.png)

You can see that the top of the heading is clipped.  If you remove the `#module-scipy.signal` id from the URL, you'll see the full heading.

That is a minor nuisance; it is the behavior of the web pages for functions where the problem becomes more signficant.  In that `scipy.signal` page, scroll down and click on, say, `filtfilt`.  The actual URL of the link is `https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/reference/generated/scipy.signal.filtfilt.html#scipy.signal.filtfilt`; note again the (redundant) reference id `#scipy.signal.filtfilt`.  Here's a screenshot:
![scipy signal filtfilt](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/321463/152663123-6a53960d-acb1-4d8b-9e87-8493369654ec.png)

Most of the signature has been cut off; all we see is the last two parameters, `method='pad', irlen=None)`.  If we scroll up, or if we remove `#scipy.signal.filtfilt` from the URL, we see what we should have seen when we clicked the link:

![scipy signal filtfilt2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/321463/152663195-3569eea8-a763-40ef-b202-92c38df960cb.png)

The docs have had this behavior for a long time.  Any chance this can be fixed?  Or is this one of those Sphinx quirks that *seems* like it should be easy to fix, but attempting to do so leads down a rabbit hole of twisty little passages...

(I usually remove the extraneous `#` id when I include a link in, say, a Stackoverflow comment or answer, to avoid the little bit of confusion that can occur when the page shows a clipped heading.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21402/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21402/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

During discussion of gh-21370, it is found that a function that computes `(1+x)**y` accurately would be helpful for accurately computing the quantile function of Tukey lambda distribution.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

Add a function `scipy.special.pow1p(x, y)`, where $x \ge -1$ and $y \in \mathbb{R}$. The function shall return $(1+x)^y$ evaluated accurately.

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

Possible formulas to evaluate $(1+x)^y$ include `pow(1+x,y)` and `exp(y*log1p(x))`. But neither formula seems to be super accurate for `x` close to (and greater than) `-0.5` and large `y`; the relative error seems to exceed `1e-14`.

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

IEEE-754 defines a similar function, `compound`, but its domain is restricted to integer `y`. I think it will be useful to support real `y`.

Doing a quick search of `pow1p` shows that R [introduced this function](https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/r-announce/2024/000701.html) in April, 2024. On other occasions, such a function is mentioned in [boost](https://www.boost.org/doc/libs/1_86_0/boost/math/special_functions/legendre.hpp) and [Julia](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/6148).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21404/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21404/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2293820554","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21404#issuecomment-2293820554","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21404","id":2293820554,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6IuOyK","user":{"login":"WarrenWeckesser","id":321463,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyMTQ2Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/321463?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser","html_url":"https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/WarrenWeckesser/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-16T16:38:36Z","updated_at":"2024-08-16T16:38:36Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"`exp(y*log1p(x))` Describe your issue.

```
python -c "import scipy.special, numpy as np;print(scipy.special.eval_hermite(np.arange(2), np.zeros((1,2), dtype=int)))"
  
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: ufunc 'eval_hermite' not supported for the input types, and the inputs could not be safely coerced to any supported types according to the casting rule ''safe''
```

One can create a GitHub Actions workflow to validate:

```yml
name: Test

on:
  push:

jobs:
  test:
    name: test scipy
    runs-on: windows-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
      with:
        python-version: "3.x"
    - run: pip install scipy
    - run: python -c "import scipy.special, numpy as np;print(scipy.special.eval_hermite(np.arange(2), np.zeros((1,2), dtype=int)))"
```

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import scipy.special, numpy as np
print(scipy.special.eval_hermite(np.arange(2), np.zeros((1,2), dtype=int)))
```


### Error message

```shell
TypeError: ufunc 'eval_hermite' not supported for the input types, and the inputs could not be safely coerced to any supported types according to the casting rule ''safe''
```


### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
scipy 1.14.0
numpy 2.0.0
python 3.12.4
Microsoft Windows Server 2022
```

CI job `Windows tests / no pythran & sdist+wheel, full, py3.11/npPre, pip+pytest` on github actions has been failing on all pull requests for a while.

The problem appears to be that some of the ufuncs in `special` have been defined to accept an argument of type `long`, which is 32 bit on Windows, but the default integer type on 64 bit Windows is now 64 bit in NumPy 2.0.  So calling those ufuncs with an array of integers with the default integer type results in an error because they can't be safely cast to `long`.

The correct solution is to update all the ufuncs to use type `npy_intp` instead of `long`; see https://numpy.org/devdocs/numpy_2_0_migration_guide.html#windows-default-integer.  This doesn't look like a simple search-and-replace operation.  It will probably involve making some updates to the ufunc code generation tool `scipy/special/_generate_pyx.py` to include the `npy_intp` type as an option.

CI job `Windows tests / no pythran & sdist+wheel, full, py3.11/npPre, pip+pytest` on github actions has been failing on all pull requests for a while.

The problem appears to be that some of the ufuncs in `special` have been defined to accept an argument of type `long`, which is 32 bit on Windows, but the default integer type on 64 bit Windows is now 64 bit in NumPy 2.0.  So calling those ufuncs with an array of integers with the default integer type results in an error because they can't be safely cast to `long`.

The correct solution is to update all the ufuncs to use type `npy_intp` instead of `long`; see https://numpy.org/devdocs/numpy_2_0_migration_guide.html#windows-default-integer.  This doesn't look like a simple search-and-replace operation.  It will probably involve making some updates to the ufunc code generation tool `scipy/special/_generate_pyx.py` to include the `npy_intp` type as an option.

Add the 'p' character to the typecodes recognized by the script `_generate_pyx.py`.  This code corresponds to the integer types `npy_intp` and `Py_ssize_t`.
* In Cython code:
  * In change the fused type `dl_number_t` to `dlp_number_t`, and include `Py_ssize_t` in the types it fuses.
  * Change other uses of `long` to `Py_ssize_t`.
* In the type signatures of ufuncs configured in `functions.json`, change occurrences of `i` or `l` in an input to `p`.
* Change use of `int` in the wrappers of `xsf` functions to `Py_ssize_t`. The thin wrappers cast this to `int` before calling the `xsf` function.
