Numerically incorrect `scipy.signal.savgol_coeffs` (also affects `scipy.signal.savgol_filter`)","user":{"login":"MothNik","id":70107495,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcwMTA3NDk1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/70107495?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik","html_url":"https://github.com/MothNik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":37334597,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzMzNDU5Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/defect","name":"defect","color":"e11d21","default":false,"description":"A clear bug or issue that prevents SciPy from being installed or used as expected"},{"id":37342572,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MjU3Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/scipy.signal","name":"scipy.signal","color":"0366d6","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-05-28T17:57:33Z","updated_at":"2024-06-03T04:09:05Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"## Basic Problem
When I ran a grid search for optimizing the Savitzky-Golay filter parameters, I found that for high `polyorders`, the coefficients from `scipy.signal.savgol_coeffs` are numerically incorrect (see **Reproducing Example**). It is very simple to check because the sum of the coefficients should be `1.0` (+- numerical tolerance).
This also affects `scipy.signal.savgol_filter` because it calls `scipy.signal.savgol_coeffs`.

## When does it occur?
I tested many `window_length`-`polyorder`-combinations and got the following plot where the circled area shows failed computations (the left dark-blue corner was not evaluated because the smoothing was not possible given too small windows for the polynomial):

![image](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/assets/70107495/418749ea-91b4-45a4-a83e-28ce1f54638d)

For higher `polyorder`s this happens for smaller `window_length`s already. `window_lengths > 100` are maybe not common, but also not completely unrealistic. For good smoothness - especially with the Savitzky-Golay filter that has the discontinuity of the filter coefficients at the edges that severely limits its noise suppression (sudden drop to 0 which is not C0- or higher -continuous) - higher polynomial degrees with long `window_length`s are the better choice (actually, the plot also indicates that because the best solution is roughly `window_length=95` for `polyorder=9` which is only slightly better than `window_length=55` for `polyorder=5`, but the first has way less residual noise because its filter edge discontinuity is less pronounced).
I also found the [following StackExchange Question](https://dsp.stackexchange.com/questions/88976/numerical-issues-in-scipys-savitzky-golay-filter-coefficients-for-large-polynom) where this problem was already pointed out together with the fact that the Matlab implementation is still capable of computing the coefficients correctly.

## What is the cause?
The problem is that `np.linalg.lstsq` is used to solve the unmodified Vandermonde-system of the polynomial fit in [this block of `scipy.signal.savgol_coeffs`](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/v1.13.1/scipy/signal/_savitzky_golay.py#L122C1-L142C34):

```python
    # Form the design matrix A. The columns of A are powers of the integers
    # from -pos to window_length - pos - 1. The powers (i.e., rows) range
    # from 0 to polyorder. (That is, A is a vandermonde matrix, but not
    # necessarily square.)
    x = np.arange(-pos, window_length - pos, dtype=float)

    if use == "conv":
        # Reverse so that result can be used in a convolution.
        x = x[::-1]

    order = np.arange(polyorder + 1).reshape(-1, 1)
    A = x ** order

    # y determines which order derivative is returned.
    y = np.zeros(polyorder + 1)
    # The coefficient assigned to y[deriv] scales the result to take into
    # account the order of the derivative and the sample spacing.
    y[deriv] = float_factorial(deriv) / (delta ** deriv)

    # Find the least-squares solution of A*c = y
    coeffs, _, _, _ = lstsq(A, y)
```

`np.linalg.lstsq` can basically cut out the singular values of an SVD of the Vandermonde-system. Since the matrix `A` becomes more and more ill-conditioned as `polyorder` and `window_length` increase, some singular values will drop below the zero-treshold which will trigger their removal. This is typical for polynomials since `A` can become imbalanced quite easily by the dominance of the high power terms.
So, `A @ coeffs = y` is not solved in a Least Squares sense anymore, but in a regularized Least Squares sense where `coeffs` with lower Euclidean norms are favoured. Such `coeffs` however do not sum up to `1.0` and the smoothing gets obscured.

## How could this be solved?
I'm currently testing out multiple solutions like [matrix balancing](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.linalg.matrix_balance.html) (probably called on LAPACK level for `np.linalg.lstsq` already) and normalization (mean subtraction and column scaling).
As a quick fix,

```python
coeffs, _, _, _ = lstsq(A, y)
```

could be changed to

```python
coeffs, _, rank, _ = lstsq(A, y)
if rank <= polyorder:  # the actual check is rank < (polyorder + 1) to be more concise
    warn("The linear system is badly conditioned, the coefficients might be incorrect.")
```

Of course, this does not really solve the problem, but warning the user reduces the risk of misuse.

Beyond Savitzky-Golay with ordinary polynomials (*monomials*), there could also be other basis functions as described in [this post](https://www.colmryan.org/posts/savitsky_golay/) .

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
### Imports ###

import numpy as np
from scipy.signal import savgol_coeffs

### Working Example ###

# this works
assert np.isclose(
    savgol_coeffs(
        window_length=35,
        polyorder=9,
    ).sum(),
    1.0,
)


### Failure Case ###

# this fails because `window_length` is too big
assert np.isclose(
    savgol_coeffs(
        window_length=175,
        polyorder=9,
    ).sum(),
    1.0,
)
```


### Error message

```shell
-
```


### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
The problem is still part of SciPy's current version 1.13.1 independent of NumPy and other system factors.
```

By using an implementation in mpmath and computing the filter coefficients with very high precision, I can confirm that the "stabilized" version produces more accurate results.  Whether or not some additional improvements can be made, we should definitely incorporate this into the function.

I noticed one correction that is needed in your code so that the `delta` parameter is handled correctly correctly.  Change this line

```
    x /= max(x_abs_max, 1.0)
```

to

```
    scale = max(x_abs_max, 1.0)
    x /= scale
    delta *= scale
```

This only matters when the `deriv` parameter is greater than 0.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2144237871/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

infrastructure","user":{"login":"mdhaber","id":6570539,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY1NzA1Mzk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6570539?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber","html_url":"https://github.com/mdhaber","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":37340722,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MDcyMg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/scipy.stats","name":"scipy.stats","color":"0366d6","default":false,"description":""},{"id":37340735,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MDczNQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/enhancement","name":"enhancement","color":"bfe5bf","default":true,"description":"A new feature or improvement"},{"id":5432239077,"node_id":"LA_kwDOABZIoc8AAAABQ8lj5Q","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/RFC","name":"RFC","color":"FEF2C0","default":false,"description":"Request for Comments; typically used to gather feedback for a substantial change proposal"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":45,"created_at":"2022-04-03T03:20:58Z","updated_at":"2024-06-03T20:47:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The existing univariate distribution infrastructure is an extremely valuable feature of SciPy, and it offers two main ways of interacting with distributions.

1. `stats.norm.pdf(2, loc=0, scale=1)` calls the `pdf` method of the `norm` object that is instantiated at import time.
2. `stats.norm(loc=0, scale=1).pdf(2)` uses the `__call__` method of the `norm` object to create a "frozen" instance of the distribution, then calls that object's `pdf` method.

There are two major advantages of approach 1 (and, to be fair, I'll be happy to add others as they come up in the comments below).

- It is a bit more natural than approach 2 for very simple invocations. For example, it may be more natural to think of the standard normal PDF as a function and call `stats.norm.pdf(2)` rather than thinking of (and instantiating) a standard normal object and invoking its `pdf` method (`stats.norm().pdf(2)`).
- It is the more common approach in existing code.

However, there are several important disadvantages.

- All ~125 distributions are instantiated upon import, increasing import time.
- Generating complete, distribution-specific documentation is impractical (gh-10328)
- Parameters are processed every time a method is called, increasing overhead (gh-17620)
- gh-13986

These could easily be avoided if `norm` were simply a _class_ (rather than an instance of the `norm_gen` class) and the user were required to instantiate that class (e.g. `norm()`) before invoking methods. This has been noted before; for example, see gh-12133 for an earlier discussion of the history and pros/cons of the two approaches.

There are also several issues that have been reported over the years that may not indicate _inherent_ disadvantages of the existing infrastructure, but do suggest that some upgrades are desired. There are several features and limitations of the existing infrasture (inflexibility w.r.t. adding/removing method arguments) that suggest that it may be easier to start fresh, reimagining the infrastructure with these enhancements in mind rather than adding patches to the existing framework. 

* gh-2509 - currently, distribution contributors need to write custom code if they want to exploit symmetry, a formula for the median, or other information about their distribution to improve speed or accuracy. I'd suggest building mechanisms for this into the infrastructure to make it easier for contributors to exploit such information and simpler for maintainers to review.
* gh-3758 - Discrete distributions should not be limited to integral support.
* gh-4538 - Every so often, we get a new question or request that indicates the demand for alternative parameterizations of distributions (e.g. gh-12134, gh-18984). I don't think it's possible to offer alternate parameterizations within the existing infrastructure without creating an entirely separate distribution class/object. We should embrace the need to offer multiple parameterizations as we design the new infrastructure.
* gh-5362 - The existing interface for fixing and providing guesses of parameters has confused users and maintainers and would be worth reimagining. On a related note, contributors should not need to entirely redefine the `fit` method to override it. The `fit` method needs a `nan_policy`/`inf_policy`.
* gh-6038 - We have a few circular distributions, but there is no infrastructure for them. This causes several problems, such as unwrapped random variates and incorrect behavior of the scale parameter. The new infrastructure should offer proper support for circular distributions. I think these would have the same name as their unwrapped counterparts, but would be selected by providing optional arguments (e.g. domain). I think it would also be reasonable to look into the computational feasibility of generic code for wrapping arbitrary distributions. 
* gh-7311 - Users want complex distributions with complex support. The new distribution interface should allow for that.
* gh-8053 - The new infrastructure will remember the random state it is assigned.
* gh-9321 - The new distribution interface should allow access to the information it knows about itself. There should also be mechanisms for modifying this information.
* gh-9439 - Because contributors cannot easily override the `loc`/`scale` parameterization, it is essentially impossible for users to fit truncated distributions with fixed truncation bounds. The new distribution interface should fix this.
* gh-10106 - The new distribution interface should make it easy to take advantage of the features of `stats.sampling` to generate random variates rather than always using inverse transform sampling (even when `ppf` is implemented numerically).
* gh-11026 - The choice of `ppf(0) = -1` and the definition of `interval` for discrete distributions should be reconsidered when planning the new interface.
* gh-11465 - Users occasionally report interest in distributions in extreme tails. The new distribution interface should offer better support for this (e.g. provide inverse of `logcdf`).
* gh-12000 - The Poisson binomial distribution is a univariate discrete distribution that does not fit well in the existing infrastructure. The new infrastructure should make it easier to implement more exotic parameterizations without needing to abandon the distribution infrastructure.
* gh-13666 - There is a lot of custom code for treating special cases of distributions (e.g. when a more general distribution simplifies to another standard distribution for special values of the shape parameters). There should be a standard mechanism for this in the new interface.
* gh-14895 - See title. Also, we should consider methods suggested in comments in `_distn_infrastructure.py` - e.g. hazard function, probability sparsity function.
* gh-15974 - We should consider implementing `relative_entropy` and `cross_entropy` in the new interface.
* gh-16877 - The use of statements like (a == a) to detect NaNs is confusing and should be avoided.
* gh-16998 - pickled/unpickled instances should behave exactly like the originals. See also gh-19524.
* gh-17307 - distributions can be frozen with invalid parameters, but `rvs` raises an error while others return NaNs
* gh-17891 - distributions accept floats for integral shape parameters, but can produce confusing or unexpected error messages (e.g. `ValueError: Integers to negative integer powers are not allowed.`) when they are used.
* gh-17973 - distribution methods are extremely slow because of all the exceptional case handling of the existing infrastructure. The exceptional case handling should be improved, and the exceptional case handling should be optional. See also gh-18519.
* gh-18265 - distributions should support object arrays with number-like dtypes
* gh-18564 - discrete distributions should be able to be unbounded below and above
* gh-18782, gh-20692 - the distribution infrastructure should take care of converting parameters to the appropriate data type to avoid these sorts of gotchas
* gh-19554 - the infrastructure should produce random variates correctly with low precision shape parameters

Starting fresh, we would also ensure that everything is designed with vectorization in mind, as we still occasionally discover issues like gh-13504, gh-13581, gh-12192, gh-11746, https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/18093#issuecomment-1468552568, gh-18919.

This enhancement proposal is for a new univariate distribution infrastructure. To be clear, I don't mean that we'd discard the old infrastructure. A lot of the existing algorithmic code would likely be retained (perhaps modernized), and the new infrastructure/distributions would generally need to pass the old tests (with updated syntax). Hopefully, the transition would be easy for users - code involving frozen distributions (approach 2 above) would need minimal changes, and the rest just needs to add some parentheses and pass arguments into the distribution initializer instead of the method itself. But we would probably rewrite parts that define methods by `exec`uting strings, for instance. (I understand it was needed at the time, but we could avoid with a new structure.)

Of course, we'd want to discuss and study other possibilities to make sure we get things right, but it might look something like this.

* Distribution families are represented by classes. The names are typically the same as existing distribution objects except in CamelCase (`norm` -> `Norm` or `Normal`, `loguniform` -> `LogUniform`).
* Distributions are instantiated before using methods. The distribution class documentation describes distribution shape parameters, which are passed as keyword-only arguments to the class `__init__` method (only). Naturally, this makes alternative parameterizations easy to support. Default support for `loc` and `scale` is provided, but distributions can disable them as needed. (Contrast against gh-15889.)
* For the most part, public methods include those that are currently available with minor changes (e.g. the name of `nnlf` is corrected to `nllf`). 
* The shape parameters used to initialize the distribution can be used as guesses for the `fit` method, which modifies the shape parameters of the distribution. Methods are available to extract the shape parameters (e.g. see gh-9321).
* There are new methods for calculating the `mode`, partial derivatives of the PDF (useful for random variate generation and fitting parameters to data), and generating the distributions of order statistics.
* As in the existing infrastructure, public methods are rarely overridden by subclasses; instead, subclasses almost exclusively override private methods. However, the new infrastructure is more strict about this distinction (e.g. there is a new `_fit` method so that fit overrides do not have to repeat boilerplate code) and there are different versions of private methods that can be overridden depending on (for example) whether the developer is able to vectorize with respect to shape parameters or would prefer to rely on a default loop.
* Methods have parameters that enable the user to control tradeoffs between precision/reliability and speed. For example, the user can opt out of expensive data validation checks. The user can opt out of special accuracy improvements (e.g. gh-2509) by specifying the desired absolute and/or relative tolerances, which can be passed into the quadrature, minimization, and root finding routines used by the generic implementations of distribution functions. The user can access accuracy estimates.
* The quadrature, minimization, and root finding routines are natively vectorized rather than requiring looping in Python.
* The infrastructure supports non-NumPy arrays (e.g. CuPy) and NumPy arrays that support nonstandard numerical types (e.g. `mpmath`).
* The infrastructure provides a standard mechanism for switching to special case implementations (e.g. t distribution to normal distribution for df=inf) rather than requiring developers to use `np.where`/`np.lazywhere`.
* Distributions "know" more about their parameters (e.g. like `_shape_info`, which was recently added to the existing infrastructure). Based on this information, parameter documentation and input validation are generated automatically.
* Formulae used in overridden methods are documented.
* The infrastructure is documented (e.g. how to create new distributions, gh-12133).
* The infrastructure natively supports truncated support, wrapped domains, folded distributions, and complex inputs (e.g. gh-17592).
* The infrastructure favors Pythonic techniques for introspection and avoids self-modification (e.g. adding methods with `exec`).
* Distribution-specific tests (gh-17807 ) assure us that the distribution object represents the distribution it is intended to represent, property-based tests (e.g. see https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/12197#issuecomment-881100965) ensure that the methods are consistent with one another and the standards for SciPy distributions, and benchmarks track the speed and accuracy (especially for numerically challenging arguments).
* The infrastructure includes a standard framework for investigating the accuracy of each method of the distribution by comparing itself with an `mpmath` backend. See notes below.
* The documentation specifies the accuracy the user can expect when using methods within a specified range of inputs (e.g. accurate to X digits for 99.Y% of parameter combinations within hypercube Z).

The new infrastructure and distributions would be widely advertised, and documentation and existing code would transition to the new infrastructure. Existing infrastructure/distributions would be supported without deprecation warnings until two releases before SciPy 2.0, at which point they would be moved to a new package that is released once (without planned maintenance).

@tupui @ev-br @tirthasheshpatel @chrisb83 

Notes on framework for investigating accuracy:
The infrastructure will accept as arguments arrays containing `mpmath.mpf` objects. Following the approach of gh-19023, special functions will dispatch to [`mparray`](https://github.com/mdhaber/mparray) versions where possible. This will allow for end-to-end calculation with arbitrary precision arithmetic and, therefore, automatic generation of reference values. By comparing numerical results computed using floating point arithmetic against these reference values, we can identify shortcomings in the implementation (e.g. ranges of parameter values where catastrophic cancellation causes loss of precision).

We cannot hope to test the accuracy for all possible floating point inputs, so we resort to random or quasi-random sampling from possible floating point inputs. From one perspective, it would be ideal if the distribution of floating point inputs tested would be representative of the distribution of floating point inputs used by users. This way, we focus limited resources on parameter ranges experienced in practice. However, this distribution is not known, so we should not make assumptions about it. Instead, I would suggest sampling parameter values uniformly from the set of all valid floating point inputs:

<details>

```python3
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
rng = np.random.default_rng(2184358452652)

types = {np.float16: np.int16, np.float32: np.int32, np.float64: np.int64,
         np.int16: np.float16, np.int32: np.float32, np.int64: np.float64}

def float2int(xf):
    """Convert the ith floating point number from zero to integer i"""
    # Adapted from numpy.testing._private.utils.integer_repr
    xf = np.asarray(xf)
    itype = types[xf.dtype.type]
    xi = xf.view(itype).copy()
    comp = np.asarray(np.iinfo(itype).min, dtype=itype)[()]
    xi[xi < 0] = comp - xi[xi < 0]
    return xi

def int2float(xi):
    """Convert integer i to the ith floating point number from zero"""
    # Adapted from numpy.testing._private.utils.integer_repr
    xi = np.asarray(xi).copy()
    comp = np.asarray(np.iinfo(xi.dtype).min, dtype=xi.dtype)[()]
    xi[xi < 0] = comp - xi[xi < 0]
    ftype = types[xi.dtype.type]
    xf = xi.view(ftype).copy()
    return xf

def sample(a, b, size, rng):
    """Sample uniformly from the set of floating point numbers between a and b"""
    int_range = float2int(np.asarray([a, b]))
    ints = rng.integers(*int_range, size=size)
    return int2float(ints)

a, b = 1e-2, 1e2
floats = sample(a, b, size=10000, rng=rng)
plt.hist(np.log10(floats))
plt.show()
```

![image](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/assets/6570539/37ea8e20-7624-4b66-8112-1a2d5524c824)

As we might expect, the floating point numbers between positive `a` and `b` are approximately log-uniformly spaced. However, the log-uniform distribution is only defined over positive support, whereas the domain of `a` and `b` above includes negative reals.

Converting a polynomial to natural window yields in unexpected behaviour: the degree of the polynomial might change.
E.g. when fitting data with a polynomial of degree 1, where data ist just constant, convert() return a degree 0 polynomial.

When implementing a fit to data, you don't know in advance, how data looks like. So fitting data with a polynomial of degree 1 you expect to get a polynomial of degree 1 in all cases. If the data is just constant, the first order coefficient is then 0 but it's not ok to remove that coefficient from output. 

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
x = np.arange(0, 10, 1)
y = 0*x + 5.32
param = np.polynomial.Polynomial.fit(x, y, 1)
param.convert().coef
```


### Error message:

_No response_

### Python and NumPy Versions:

1.26.4
3.12.0 

### Runtime Environment:

_No response_

### Context for the issue:

Fitting a polynomial is a standard task. > MSVC is not advanced enough to create arrays on stack which have sizes dependent on variables

Pedantic clarification: a declaration such as `std::complex<double> cwrk[n];` is known in the C world as a variable length array (VLA).  These are allowed in C99.  But `amos.h` is a C++ file, and variable length arrays are not allowed in *standard* C++.  If it compiles, it is because the compiler provides a nonstandard extension.  MSVC does not provide this extension.

With `g++` you can detect this with, for example, the command line option `-pedantic-errors`, which will tell you that you are trying to use a C99 feature that is not part of C++. For example,

`foo.cpp`:

```
void foo(int n) {
    int x[n];
}
```

```
% g++ -pedantic-errors -c foo.cpp
foo.cpp:2:11: error: variable length arrays are a C99 feature [-Werror,-Wvla-extension]
    int x[n];
          ^
1 error generated.
```

When given a string, it looks like `np.clongdouble()` is parsing the numeric values as `float64` and losing precision in the conversion.  For example, in the following, I expect `np.longdouble(s)` and `np.clongdouble(s).real` to give the same result:

```
In [73]: s = "0.333333333333333333333333"

In [74]: np.longdouble(s)
Out[74]: np.longdouble('0.33333333333333333334')

In [75]: np.clongdouble(s).real
Out[75]: np.longdouble('0.33333333333333331483')

``` 
Instead, `clongdouble()` appears to be parsing to `float64`, c.f.

```
In [78]: np.longdouble(np.float64(s))
Out[78]: np.longdouble('0.33333333333333331483')
```

This is on a Linux machine where the `long double` type is 80 bit extended precision.

```python
See above.
```

Mailing list should usually be notified."}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-06-17T18:35:19Z","updated_at":"2024-06-17T18:35:20Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

I'm working on improving an existing ufunc (complex `log1p`) in numpy, and I'm implementing the updated version in C++.  The function will use several functions from `npy_math` (`npy_log`, `npy_log1p`, `npy_fabs`, `npy_atan2`, `npy_hypot`), and these all follow the C convention of having three versions, one for each of the types `npy_float`, `npy_double` and `npy_longdouble`, e.g. `npy_fabsf`, `npy_fabs` and `npy_fabsl`.

It would really nice if we had a C++ namespace (e.g. `npy` to match the "poor man's namespace" implemented with the prefix `npy_` in C) that provides a single name with overloaded implementations for each type, e.g. `npy::fabs`.  This would make writing templated code in C++ *much* easier.

As an experiment, I added this code to the end of `npy_math.h`:

```
#ifdef __cplusplus

//
// Trivial C++ wrappers for several npy_* functions.
//
#define CPP_WRAP1(name) \
inline npy_float name(const npy_float x)                \
{                                                       \
    return ::npy_ ## name ## f(x);                      \
}                                                       \
inline npy_double name(const npy_double x)              \
{                                                       \
    return ::npy_ ## name(x);                           \
}                                                       \
inline npy_longdouble name(const npy_longdouble x)      \
{                                                       \
    return ::npy_ ## name ## l(x);                      \
}                                                       \


#define CPP_WRAP2(name) \
inline npy_float name(const npy_float x,                \
                      const npy_float y)                \
{                                                       \
    return ::npy_ ## name ## f(x, y);                   \
}                                                       \
inline npy_double name(const npy_double x,              \
                       const npy_double y)              \
{                                                       \
    return ::npy_ ## name(x, y);                        \
}                                                       \
inline npy_longdouble name(const npy_longdouble x,      \
                           const npy_longdouble y)      \
{                                                       \
    return ::npy_ ## name ## l(x, y);                   \
}                                                       \

namespace npy {

CPP_WRAP1(fabs)
CPP_WRAP1(log)
CPP_WRAP1(log1p)
CPP_WRAP2(atan2)
CPP_WRAP2(hypot)

}

#endif // __cplusplus
```

Then in a templated C++ function that includes `npy_math.h`, I use `npy::fabs`, `npy::log`, etc.  This works fine.

Is there interest in such an update to `npy_math.h` (or some other header) to facilitate writing code in C++?  Of course, giving all the functions in `npy_math.h` similar wrappers will be more work, and there might be some gotchas that show up in the process, but with more and more code being implemented in C++ in NumPy, I think this is something we need.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26729/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26729/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

`scipy.signal.savgol_filter`)","user":{"login":"MothNik","id":70107495,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcwMTA3NDk1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/70107495?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik","html_url":"https://github.com/MothNik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MothNik/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":37334597,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzMzNDU5Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/defect","name":"defect","color":"e11d21","default":false,"description":"A clear bug or issue that prevents SciPy from being installed or used as expected"},{"id":37342572,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MjU3Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/scipy.signal","name":"scipy.signal","color":"0366d6","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2024-05-28T17:57:33Z","updated_at":"2024-06-18T14:37:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"## Basic Problem
When I ran a grid search for optimizing the Savitzky-Golay filter parameters, I found that for high `polyorders`, the coefficients from `scipy.signal.savgol_coeffs` are numerically incorrect (see **Reproducing Example**). It is very simple to check because the sum of the coefficients should be `1.0` (+- numerical tolerance).
This also affects `scipy.signal.savgol_filter` because it calls `scipy.signal.savgol_coeffs`.

## When does it occur?
I tested many `window_length`-`polyorder`-combinations and got the following plot where the circled area shows failed computations (the left dark-blue corner was not evaluated because the smoothing was not possible given too small windows for the polynomial):

![image](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/assets/70107495/418749ea-91b4-45a4-a83e-28ce1f54638d)

For higher `polyorder`s this happens for smaller `window_length`s already. `window_lengths > 100` are maybe not common, but also not completely unrealistic. For good smoothness - especially with the Savitzky-Golay filter that has the discontinuity of the filter coefficients at the edges that severely limits its noise suppression (sudden drop to 0 which is not C0- or higher -continuous) - higher polynomial degrees with long `window_length`s are the better choice (actually, the plot also indicates that because the best solution is roughly `window_length=95` for `polyorder=9` which is only slightly better than `window_length=55` for `polyorder=5`, but the first has way less residual noise because its filter edge discontinuity is less pronounced).
I also found the [following StackExchange Question](https://dsp.stackexchange.com/questions/88976/numerical-issues-in-scipys-savitzky-golay-filter-coefficients-for-large-polynom) where this problem was already pointed out together with the fact that the Matlab implementation is still capable of computing the coefficients correctly.

## What is the cause?
The problem is that `np.linalg.lstsq` is used to solve the unmodified Vandermonde-system of the polynomial fit in [this block of `scipy.signal.savgol_coeffs`](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/v1.13.1/scipy/signal/_savitzky_golay.py#L122C1-L142C34):

```python
    # Form the design matrix A. The columns of A are powers of the integers
    # from -pos to window_length - pos - 1. The powers (i.e., rows) range
    # from 0 to polyorder. (That is, A is a vandermonde matrix, but not
    # necessarily square.)
    x = np.arange(-pos, window_length - pos, dtype=float)

    if use == "conv":
        # Reverse so that result can be used in a convolution.
        x = x[::-1]

    order = np.arange(polyorder + 1).reshape(-1, 1)
    A = x ** order

    # y determines which order derivative is returned.
    y = np.zeros(polyorder + 1)
    # The coefficient assigned to y[deriv] scales the result to take into
    # account the order of the derivative and the sample spacing.
    y[deriv] = float_factorial(deriv) / (delta ** deriv)

    # Find the least-squares solution of A*c = y
    coeffs, _, _, _ = lstsq(A, y)
```

`np.linalg.lstsq` can basically cut out the singular values of an SVD of the Vandermonde-system. Since the matrix `A` becomes more and more ill-conditioned as `polyorder` and `window_length` increase, some singular values will drop below the zero-treshold which will trigger their removal. This is typical for polynomials since `A` can become imbalanced quite easily by the dominance of the high power terms.
So, `A @ coeffs = y` is not solved in a Least Squares sense anymore, but in a regularized Least Squares sense where `coeffs` with lower Euclidean norms are favoured. Such `coeffs` however do not sum up to `1.0` and the smoothing gets obscured.

## How could this be solved?
I'm currently testing out multiple solutions like [matrix balancing](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.linalg.matrix_balance.html) (probably called on LAPACK level for `np.linalg.lstsq` already) and normalization (mean subtraction and column scaling).
As a quick fix,

```python
coeffs, _, _, _ = lstsq(A, y)
```

could be changed to

```python
coeffs, _, rank, _ = lstsq(A, y)
if rank <= polyorder:  # the actual check is rank < (polyorder + 1) to be more concise
    warn("The linear system is badly conditioned, the coefficients might be incorrect.")
```

Of course, this does not really solve the problem, but warning the user reduces the risk of misuse.

Beyond Savitzky-Golay with ordinary polynomials (*monomials*), there could also be other basis functions as described in [this post](https://www.colmryan.org/posts/savitsky_golay/) .

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
### Imports ###

import numpy as np
from scipy.signal import savgol_coeffs

### Working Example ###

# this works
assert np.isclose(
    savgol_coeffs(
        window_length=35,
        polyorder=9,
    ).sum(),
    1.0,
)


### Failure Case ###

# this fails because `window_length` is too big
assert np.isclose(
    savgol_coeffs(
        window_length=175,
        polyorder=9,
    ).sum(),
    1.0,
)
```


### Error message

```shell
-
```


### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
The problem is still part of SciPy's current version 1.13.1 independent of NumPy and other system factors.
```

In particular, I experimented a bit with your stabilized version, and with the simple method (mentioned in the comments of `savgol_coeffs()`) of using numpy's polynomial fitting to compute the coefficients. I added a translation of `savgol_coeffs()` to `mpmath` in my [`mpsig`](https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser/mpsig) repository: [`_savitzky_golay`](https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser/mpsig/blob/main/mpsig/_savitzky_golay.py).  I didn't include scaling, but since it is using mpmath, one can just crank up the precision as high as one needs.

I use that in the script [`savgol_work.py`](https://github.com/WarrenWeckesser/experiments/blob/main/python/scipy/savgol-precision/savgol_work.py) to compare the relative error of the coefficients computed with the stabilized version to a simple version that uses numpy's `Polynomial.fit()`. (I didn't try to implement all the parameters of `savgol_coeffs()` in that function.)  This plot show an example where the version based on `Polynomial.fit()` performs much better than the stabilized version.  This example was cherry-picked to show how big the difference can be. Typically the `Polynomial.fit()` version is similar to or just a little better than the stabilized version.  Note that both versions are significantly better than the existing version, so I didn't include it in the plot.

![savgol_coeffs_relerr](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/assets/321463/745f2b7a-2f06-43b6-8d9d-e5ef7d79be7d)

I probably won't get back to this for another week or so. Mailing list should usually be notified."},{"id":3276642361,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzMjc2NjQyMzYx","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/C++","name":"C++","color":"395262","default":false,"description":"Related to introduction and use of C++ in the NumPy code base"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-06-17T18:35:19Z","updated_at":"2024-06-18T16:58:01Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

I'm working on improving an existing ufunc (complex `log1p`) in numpy, and I'm implementing the updated version in C++.  The function will use several functions from `npy_math` (`npy_log`, `npy_log1p`, `npy_fabs`, `npy_atan2`, `npy_hypot`), and these all follow the C convention of having three versions, one for each of the types `npy_float`, `npy_double` and `npy_longdouble`, e.g. `npy_fabsf`, `npy_fabs` and `npy_fabsl`.

It would really nice if we had a C++ namespace (e.g. `npy` to match the "poor man's namespace" implemented with the prefix `npy_` in C) that provides a single name with overloaded implementations for each type, e.g. `npy::fabs`.  This would make writing templated code in C++ *much* easier.

As an experiment, I added this code to the end of `npy_math.h`:

```
#ifdef __cplusplus

//
// Trivial C++ wrappers for several npy_* functions.
//
#define CPP_WRAP1(name) \
inline npy_float name(const npy_float x)                \
{                                                       \
    return ::npy_ ## name ## f(x);                      \
}                                                       \
inline npy_double name(const npy_double x)              \
{                                                       \
    return ::npy_ ## name(x);                           \
}                                                       \
inline npy_longdouble name(const npy_longdouble x)      \
{                                                       \
    return ::npy_ ## name ## l(x);                      \
}                                                       \


#define CPP_WRAP2(name) \
inline npy_float name(const npy_float x,                \
                      const npy_float y)                \
{                                                       \
    return ::npy_ ## name ## f(x, y);                   \
}                                                       \
inline npy_double name(const npy_double x,              \
                       const npy_double y)              \
{                                                       \
    return ::npy_ ## name(x, y);                        \
}                                                       \
inline npy_longdouble name(const npy_longdouble x,      \
                           const npy_longdouble y)      \
{                                                       \
```

My immediate need is for use within numpy--I'm working on improving the `log1p` ufunc.  Templating with C++ is nicer than templating with .src.  For now, I'll put the minimal set of C++ wrappers that I need in the file that implements the complex `log1p` function, and not touch `npy_math.h`.  We can always expand the (private, internal) C++ API later if it turns out to be more generally useful.

> I don't see any reason why we'd want more people using numpy's version of functions that are provided by C99/C++11 and up. Use std::fabs, not npy::fabs.

print(xy == xxyy)

print("NP VERSION", np.version.version)
